title: feat: container-aware idle tracking for socket-activated API service
body:
### Feature request description

I run `podman system service` through systemd socket activation, and the API process exits while containers are still around because the idle tracker only looks at HTTP connections when deciding if the service is "idle".

In my workflow, a client creates a container, streams its logs via the API, and then calls `/containers/{id}/wait` after the log stream closes. But by the time it does, the API has already shut down (5s default timeout, 0 active HTTP connections) and the fresh process socket-activated for the `wait` call returns a 404.

The `IdleTracker` in `pkg/api/server/idle/tracker.go` tracks `managed` HTTP connections and `hijacked` connections (attach/exec). When `ActiveConnections()` hits 0 the idle countdown starts. Running containers don't factor in at all. The shutdown path in `server.go` waits on `idleTracker.Done()` and calls `Shutdown(false)` without checking for running containers.

### Suggest potential solution

The idle tracker should be aware of running containers. If containers started through the API are still running, the service shouldn't consider itself idle.

Podman already has an internal events system that fires on container start/die/remove. The API server could subscribe to those events and maintain a counter. `ActiveConnections()` would become `len(managed) + hijacked + containers`, and the idle timer would only start when everything is at zero.

On startup, the tracker should also seed the counter by querying currently running containers so it handles containers that outlived a previous API process (thanks to `KillMode=process`).

This doesn't touch the Docker-compatible API surface. It just keeps the API process alive while it has work to serve, which is what clients already assume.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Setting `service_timeout=0` works but disables idle termination entirely and loses the point of socket activation. The other option is to have the client hold a `wait` connection open alongside the log stream so there's always an active HTTP connection, but that's a quirk every API client has to learn about.

### Additional context

There are a couple of older issues that dealt with symptoms of the same gap:

- #7294 fixed containers getting killed on API shutdown with `KillMode=process`
- #7826 fixed the timer firing despite active HTTP connections

Both are resolved. Neither made the idle tracker aware of container lifecycle.

Environment: Podman 5.8.1, NixOS 26.05, cgroup v2, systemd, rootless.

comments.body:
> In my workflow, a client creates a container, streams its logs via the API, and then calls /containers/{id}/wait after the log stream closes. But by the time it does, the API has already shut down (5s default timeout, 0 active HTTP connections) and the fresh process socket-activated for the wait call returns a 404.

And what exactly is the issue? The 404 has nothing to do whenever the service is up or not it means the container was already removed. The service is not a daemon is there is zero need to have it up when a container is running, keeping the service running will not change the API responses.


> The other option is to have the client hold a wait connection open alongside the log stream so there's always an active HTTP connection, but that's a quirk every API client has to learn about.

Yes if you remove the container automatically you need to do that, and that is what podman-remote does. Doing it in serial never can work because by the time the wait call comes in the container might already be removed, or restarted and no longer gives you the current exit code result. 


Given that I see no reason to implement that
